critic_raw: The TFT vs. gradient boosting load forecasting experiment is carefully designed. I want to probe the weather forecast input strategy. The design uses 'next-day forecast weather (using the forecast available at the time each prediction was made, not hindsight weather).' This is correct for avoiding lookahead bias. However, weather forecast quality varies significantly between seasons and by lead time. In summer, solar radiation forecasts may be more uncertain than in winter. If the TFT better handles uncertain forecast inputs (through its uncertainty quantification capabilities), it may outperform gradient boosting not because of better architecture but because forecast uncertainty is higher in certain periods that happen to dominate the test set. Additionally, the design retrains on training+validation combined before final test evaluation. This means the TFT is trained on more data than was used to select its hyperparameters — the optimal hyperparameters for the combined dataset may differ from those selected on the training-only dataset.
defender_raw: On weather forecast uncertainty: both models receive the same forecast inputs, so forecast quality variation affects both equally. If the TFT better handles uncertain inputs, this IS an architectural advantage that should count. The seasonal MAE secondary metric explicitly tests whether the TFT's advantage is concentrated in specific seasons. If the advantage is uniform across summer/winter/spring/fall, it is genuine. On retraining on combined data with training-only hyperparameters: this is standard practice. The hyperparameters selected on the training-validation split are already regularized to prevent overfitting; adding validation data provides more signal without changing the regularization scheme. Both models are retrained on the same combined dataset with the same frozen hyperparameters, preserving comparative fairness. The test set evaluation provides an unbiased estimate of final performance.
adjudication_raw: Two concerns: weather forecast uncertainty seasonality and retraining with training-only hyperparameters. Both addressed: forecast quality varies symmetrically for both models; seasonal MAE is a secondary metric; retraining with combined data is standard practice applied equally to both. Verdict: defense_wins.
